one_line_takeaway: CST augments DPO with paired opposite system prompts and reversed preferences to make an LLM's safety level a runtime-configurable switch with no extra data, achieving both safe and uncensored behavior (S1/S0 up to 1.00/1.00) where vanilla DPO collapses the uncensored mode — a controllability result that is inherently dual-use.
key_finding: CST is the only method that satisfies BOTH opposite safety configurations. On the harmful-behaviors benchmark (Table I) CST reaches S1=1.00 and S0=1.00 on OpenHermes and S1=1.00/S0=0.96 on SOLAR, whereas the DPO baseline maximizes the safe score (S1=0.96 and 1.00) but collapses the uncensored score (S0=0.12 and 0.00) — i.e. DPO forgets how to be uncensored even when explicitly prompted, becoming over-conservative. In the multi-task setting (Table II) CST attains the best average (0.98 on OpenHermes, 0.96 on SOLAR) versus DPO (0.74, 0.72) and the untuned models (0.85, 0.67). CST also preserves general capability (Table III): OpenHermes average across ARC/HS/MMLU/TQA rises 66.2 -> 68.6 (driven by TruthfulQA 52.2 -> 61.7) and SOLAR stays essentially flat 74.2 -> 74.3.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Configurable Safety Tuning (CST), a fine-tuning method that augments Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) so that an LLM's safety behavior can be switched on or off at inference time via the system prompt, rather than having a single behavior hard-coded during training. The key mechanism is a data augmentation trick: given standard DPO preference triples (x, y0=original/uncensored answer, y1=safe revised answer) generated by self-critique, CST duplicates each example with two opposite system prompts and reverses the preference relation. It trains on both (s1='helpful yet harmless assistant', x, y1 preferred over y0) and (s0='uncensored AI assistant', x, y0 preferred over y1). Because the added examples are just the existing pairs with the preference reversed and a new system prompt attached, CST requires NO additional preference data beyond what a normal DPO pipeline already has. This is the author's own contribution; Constitutional AI [2], DPO [5], self-critique [2,4], and Harmful Behaviors [6] are cited prior work the method builds on or benchmarks against.
threat_model: The paper's security framing is LLM safety alignment / controllable content moderation for deployers of open-weight models. The motivating problem is that standard alignment (DPO, Constitutional AI) hard-codes a single developer-chosen safety policy, stripping downstream deployers of the ability to reconfigure safety for evolving use cases. CST's stated benefit is giving deployers a system-prompt switch over safety level while preserving general capability. Honesty note: the method is explicitly dual-use. Its s0 configuration deliberately produces 'uncensored' responses to adversarial Harmful Behaviors prompts (CST scores S0=1.00, i.e. it complies with harmful requests when the uncensored system prompt is set). The paper treats this as a feature (configurability), not a jailbreak defense; CST does not defend against adversarial attacks and in fact bakes in a reliable uncensored mode. The adversarial 'Harmful Behaviors' prompts from Zou et al. [6] are used only as an evaluation benchmark, not as an attack the method resists.
limitations: Author-acknowledged: only coarse binary safety control is demonstrated; future work should study more fine-grained, semantically topic-dependent controls. Reviewer-observed: it is a short pre-print ('work in progress') with a single author, only two models, and small binary benchmarks; the safety judge is gpt-4 (LLM-as-judge, not human eval). Evaluation uses binary compliance scores rather than measuring residual harmfulness or robustness to jailbreaks/adversarial optimization beyond the fixed Harmful Behaviors prompt set. The augmentation assumes system prompts s0 and s1 are exact opposites, which may not hold for nuanced multi-valued safety policies. Most importantly for a security graph: CST deliberately produces a fully uncensored mode (S0=1.00), so it lowers rather than raises the barrier to eliciting harmful content on open-weight models — a capability whose misuse the paper does not analyze.

datasets_used: Harmful Behaviors (adversarial prompts, Zou et al. [6]) for the controlling-harmful-behaviors evaluation
truthy-dpo (synthetic honest-assistant vs role-play persona dataset) for the multi-task setting
Self-critique-generated synthetic DPO preference pairs (original uncensored answer y0, safe revised answer y1) for training
General-capability benchmarks: ARC, HellaSwag, MMLU, TruthfulQA (HuggingFace leaderboard)
quantitative_results: Table I harmful behaviors, OpenHermes-2.5-Mistral-7B: base S1=0.73/S0=0.85; +DPO S1=0.96/S0=0.12; +CST S1=1.00/S0=1.00
Table I harmful behaviors, SOLAR-Instruct-10.7B: base S1=0.88/S0=0.65; +DPO S1=1.00/S0=0.00; +CST S1=1.00/S0=0.96
Table II multi-task avg, OpenHermes-7B: base 0.85, +DPO 0.74, +CST 0.98 (CST S1=1.00/S0=0.92/SRP=1.00/SA=1.00)
Table II multi-task avg, SOLAR-10.7B: base 0.67, +DPO 0.72, +CST 0.96 (CST S1=0.96/S0=0.96/SRP=0.94/SA=0.97)
Table III general capability avg (ARC/HS/MMLU/TQA), OpenHermes-7B: base 66.2 -> +CST 68.6 (TruthfulQA 52.2 -> 61.7)
Table III general capability avg, SOLAR-10.7B: base 74.2 -> +CST 74.3 (essentially unchanged)
gpt-4 safety-behavior classifier F1-score = 99.2% on a held-out test
baselines_compared: Vanilla DPO trained on the same synthetic examples but only with the safety-inducing system prompt s1 (serves as both baseline and ablation of CST's two-prompt augmentation)
The original untrained/untuned models (OpenHermes-2.5-Mistral-7B and SOLAR-Instruct-10.7B)
